items.correct: An object's design should be determined by its purpose.
items.misconception: Decoration is the most important aspect of design.
items.random_wrong: Designers must follow strict government forms.
items.why: Students invert the meaning. Sullivan's modernist principle prioritises FUNCTION over ornament - the chair's shape should serve sitting, not the other way around. Postmodernism later challenged this.

items.correct: The small projecting line at the end of letter strokes.
items.misconception: A whole typeface that looks formal.
items.why: Students conflate the FEATURE (the small foot/stroke) with the CLASSIFICATION (serif typefaces, which have them). Sans serif means 'without serifs' - those little projections at letter endings.

items.q: What is the significance of Duchamp's 'Fountain'?
items.correct: It challenged the definition of art - the artist's choice, not their skill, becomes the artwork.
items.random_wrong: It is a study of plumbing.
items.why: Students miss the conceptual point. Duchamp didn't make the urinal - he selected and presented it. The radical claim is that ART IS DESIGNATION, opening the door to all conceptual art.

items.q: Which traditional Indonesian technique uses wax to resist dye?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Batik. “Shibori” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which contemporary British artist is known for autobiographical embroidered blankets?
items.why: Correct answer: Tracey Emin. “Grayson Perry” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many sets of threads make up a basic woven fabric (warp and weft)?
items.why: Exact answer: 2 thread sets. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
